draft.introduction: Genesis 22:1–19 recounts the binding of Isaac (the Akedah), where God commands Abraham to sacrifice his son. The test of radical obedience and the last-moment reprieve became paradigmatic for Jewish and Christian theology alike. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And it came to pass after these things that God tested Abraham, and said to him, “Abraham, Abraham!” And he said, “Here I am.” And he said, “Take your beloved son, whom you love, Isaac, and go to the high land, and offer him there as a whole burnt offering on one of the mountains that I will tell you.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο μετὰ τὰ ὅήματα ταῦτα ὁ θεὸς ἐπείρασεν τὸν Αβρααμ

translation.text: And an angel of the Lord called to him from heaven, and said, “Abraham, Abraham!” And he said, “Here I am.” And he said, “Do not lay your hand on the child, and do nothing to him; for now I know that you fear God, and you have not spared your beloved son for my sake.” And Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught in a thicket by its horns, and he offered it as a whole burnt offering instead of Isaac his son.
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐκάλεσεν αὐτὸν ἄγγελος κυρίου ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ

translator_notes.note: The Septuagint renders “your beloved son” (τὸν υἱόν σου τὸν ἀγαπητόν) using the same word (agapetos) that appears at Jesus’s baptism: “This is my beloved Son.” The early Christians read the Akedah as a type of the crucifixion: the father offering the beloved son, with the ram as a figure of the substitute sacrifice.
